docs.h: The Atharvan roots — the oldest layer · The Vedic → tantric continuity · The abhicāra (ābhicārika) hymns · The counter-sorcery — kṛtyāpratiharaṇa, pratyabhicāra & cātana · What a kṛtyā is
docs.b: Home › Traditions › Abhicāra › The Atharvan roots The Atharvan roots — the oldest layer Long before the tantric ṣaṭkarman systematized the six acts, the Atharvaveda (the Śaunaka Saṃhitā, c. 1200-1000 BCE) already held a large corpus of abhicāra hymns — charms against enemies, sorcerers and demons — together with the kṛtyā counter-magic that turns a spell back on its maker. The Kauśika Sūtra is the manual that tells you which hymn belongs to which rite. This is the stratum the later tantras inherited and reorganized. Described as history — the frame holds here too These hymns are quoted only in their public-domain scholarly translations (Bloomfield 1897; Whitney-Lanman 1905), by short title and purpose, exactly as an Indology reference would list them. The Kauśika Sūtra numbers say which hymn a rite used ; they are bibliographic pointers, not a set of instructions . Nothing here has any demonstrated validity, and nothing here tells anyone how to do anything. The Vedic → tantric continuity The through-line is direct. The Atharvan sorcerer's toolkit — hymns to destroy an enemy , to eject a rival , to make a man impotent , to expose and burn hidden wizards — is the same conceptual space the medieval tantras would later carve into vaśīkaraṇa , uccāṭana , māraṇa and the rest, with their seasons and colours and gestures. And the Vedic layer already carried an expiation : Atharvaveda 16.2, recited while washing the whole body, "for removing all sins committed during the performance of the ritual" (Kauśika Sūtra 49.27, the closing sūtra of the witchcraft section). The defensive half of the corpus — the kṛtyāpratiharaṇa ("repelling of witchcraft") and cātana ("expelling") charms — is just as old as the aggressive half. It presents them exactly as the academic literature does — as religious history and belief-system analysis — and nothing more. Please read this first — the honest frame These are historical, religious symbolic systems with no demonstrated validity. Everything here is described, never prescribed . "Abhicāra" is the tradition's own word for hostile ritual; "black magic" is a colonial-era and modern-popular gloss , not the texts' own category, and this wing keeps the scholarly frame instead. The māraṇa-class ("slaying") material states only what the texts CLAIM , as history — there are no dosages, no step-by-step rites, and no "how to harm" anywhere in this wing. What you will find are the same catalogued correspondence tables that Princeton University Press and Motilal Banarsidass themselves print — colours, seasons, gestures, rosary materials — plus the calendar arithmetic of when the tradition said each act "belonged". This page exists to explain a historical belief-system; reading it, or computing the timing screen, does nothing in the world. Why this belongs on the Workbench The rest of this site computes the astrology of Lilly and the Picatrix as history made calculable , and it already carries the Latin magical corpus — the planetary talismans, the decan images, the kameas — as catalogued data with citations, never as working spells. docs.b: Home › Traditions › Abhicāra › The six acts The six acts, one by one The tantric texts systematized ritual magic into the six acts — the ṣaṭkarman — and then hung an elaborate lattice of correspondences on them: each act has its season, its hour, its direction, its deity, its colour, its posture-mat, its gesture, its rosary and its finger-hold. This page lays out the verified tables in full, act by act, shows the places where the texts contradict themselves (flagged ⚑, never resolved), and lets you cast the timing screen for any moment. Described, never prescribed Every table below is catalogued data , presented as the scholarship presents it — the same colour, season and gesture lists that Princeton UP and Motilal print. None of it has any demonstrated validity, and none of it is an instruction . The māraṇa ("slaying") section carries only the symbolic and classificatory correspondences and states what the texts CLAIM, as religious history — there is no procedure here that could function as a rite. Always "six" — even when it isn't The group is called ṣaṭkarmāṇi ("six acts") almost universally, but the actual lists differ text to text — a text's index verse will promise six while its body treats seven or eight. "Index verses differing from the actual contents is common, if not universal, in the magic tantras" (Ullrey). The site keeps every variant; it never silently picks one. List Items The canonical six Mahīdhara, MMU 25 śānti · vaśīkaraṇa · stambhana · vidveṣaṇa · uccāṭana · māraṇa (appeasement, subjugation, immobilization, enmity, eradication, liquidation) Sivadatta's Uḍḍīśatantra index (6) vs body (7) Index verse (U 1.16): śānti · vaśya · stambhana · vidveṣa · uccāṭana · māraṇa. Body (7, killing first, no śānti): māraṇa · mohana (bewildering) · stambhana · vidveṣa

docs.b: Home › Traditions › Abhicāra › Sources The texts & the scholarship The Indian ritual-magic tradition is a dated chain of texts — from the Atharvaveda's Vedic charms through the medieval tantric compendia to Mahīdhara's late systematization of 1588 — read through a century of critical scholarship. This page lists every source with its editions and its attribution flags , then states plainly what this wing verified — including the former direction/deity gap, now closed. Date = the standard consensus range. Kauśika Sūtra Vedic ritual manual the ābhicārika-gaṇa & cātana-gaṇa (which hymn is used in which rite) ed. Bloomfield, JAOS 14 (1890); applications trans. Caland (1900). Prapañcasāra (attrib. Śaṅkara) c. 10th-11th c. six-rites parallel at 446-452 ⚑ the Śaṅkara attribution is traditional — Bühnemann calls the text "anonymous". docs.b: Home › Reference › About & Sources About, Sources & Scientific Context This project is a study edition: faithful to Lilly's text, honest about the science, and transparent about its methods. Contents: Lilly · the book & editions · read it free · the modern revival · the science · internal debates · technical notes · sources . William Lilly (1602–1681) Born 1 May 1602 at Diseworth, Leicestershire, the son of a yeoman, Lilly walked to London in 1620 and rose from servant to the foremost astrologer of his age. He took up the art in 1632, and from 1641 practised professionally, casting (by his own account) close to two thousand questions a year. His annual almanacs under the byline Merlinus Anglicus (from 1644) sold tens of thousands of copies and made him a real force in the propaganda of the English Civil War, in which he sided with Parliament. His coded “hieroglyphic” images of a burning city, published in Monarchy or No Monarchy (1651), led to his being examined by a parliamentary committee after the Great Fire of London in 1666 — he was discharged, with the help of his friend Elias Ashmole. He died at Hersham, Surrey, on 9 June 1681. His candid autobiography, addressed to Ashmole, was published in 1715. The book and its editions Christian Astrology, Modestly Treated of in Three Books (London, 1647) was the first comprehensive textbook of astrology written in English rather than Latin. It was printed by Thomas Brudenell for John Partridge and Humphrey Blunden. A second edition , “corrected and amended,” followed in 1659 (printed by John Macock) — substantively a corrected reprint. Planet Sign Degree (0–29) Sect Day chart Night chart How essential dignity works A planet is dignified when it occupies a sign or degree it has rulership over. Lilly recognises five, in descending strength: domicile (+5), exaltation (+4), triplicity (+3), term (+2) and face (+1). A planet in the sign opposite its domicile is in detriment (−5); opposite its exaltation it is in fall (−4). A planet with no dignity of its own at all is peregrine (−5) — “wandering”, like a stranger with no standing. “A Planet is then said to be really strong when he hath many essentiall dignities… in his house, exaltation, triplicity, terme or face, at time of the erecting of the figure.” — Lilly, Christian Astrology, Book I The full Table of Essential Dignities Accidental dignity & debility Where essential dignity measures a planet's quality, accidental dignity measures its situation — its house, motion, and relationship to the Sun and to the benefics, malefics and fixed stars. Lilly's table (Book I, p.115): Accidental dignity Pts In the 1st or 10th house +5 In the 7th, 4th or 11th +4 In the 2nd or 5th +3 In the 9th +2 In the 3rd +1 Direct +4 Swift in motion +2 ☄ Oriental (♄♃♂) / Occidental (☿♀) +2 Free from the Sun's beams +5 Cazimi (heart of the Sun) +5 Partile ☌ Jupiter or Venus +5 ☌ Regulus (24° Leo) +6 ☌ Spica (18° Libra) +5 Accidental debility Pts In the 12th house −5 In the 8th or 6th −2 Retrograde −5 Slow in motion −2 Combust the Sun −5 Under the Sun's beams −4 Partile ☌ Saturn or Mars −5 Besieged by Saturn & Mars −5 Partile □ or ☍ Saturn/Mars −4 ☌ Caput Algol

docs.h: Planetary Hours · How it works
docs.b: Home › Cast › Planetary Hours Planetary Hours The day is divided into twelve equal “hours” between sunrise and sunset, and the night into twelve between sunset and the next sunrise — so the hours are unequal , longer by day in summer. Each is ruled by a planet in the Chaldean order, beginning at sunrise with the ruler of the weekday. Place Latitude °N Longitude °E Date Today Show hours How it works The Chaldean order arranges the planets from slowest to fastest: Saturn ♄ · Jupiter ♃ · Mars ♂ · Sun ☉ · Venus ♀ · Mercury ☿ · Moon ☽ . The planetary day begins not at midnight but at sunrise ; the first hour is ruled by the planet of the weekday (Sun→Sunday, Moon→Monday, Mars→Tuesday, Mercury→Wednesday, Jupiter→Thursday, Venus→Friday, Saturn→Saturday). Each following hour steps one place along the Chaldean order. Why the days are named as they are Because 24 hours leave a remainder of three when divided among the seven planets, each day's first hour falls three places further along the Chaldean ring — which is exactly the order of the weekdays. The same Chaldean order also rules the faces (decans) of the zodiac. Sunrise/sunset are computed astronomically for the place and date. In horary, Lilly checks whether the planetary hour-ruler agrees with the ruler of the Ascendant as a test of the figure's radicality — see the Horary Calculator .

docs.b: Home › Traditions › Book II — Horary › Considerations before Judgement Considerations before Judgement Before reading a horary chart, Lilly “gives warning to the Astrologer” to consider whether the figure is radical — fit to be judged. These are cautions for reflection, not mechanical rules that forbid a reading. “The Question then shall be taken for radicall, or fit to be judged, when as the Lord of the hour … and the Lord of the Ascendant or first House, are of one Triplicity, or be one, or of the same nature.” — Lilly, Christian Astrology, Book II The considerations The Ascendant in early degrees If the first three degrees of a sign ascend, “the matter is not yet ripe for judgement” — unless the querent is very young or the matter genuinely new. The Ascendant in late degrees If 27°–29° ascend, “it's no wayes safe to give judgement” — the matter may be already decided, or out of the querent's hands. (Lilly allows an exception when the querent's age matches the degree.) The Moon void of course When the Moon makes no further aspect before leaving her sign, “all manner of matters go hardly on … yet somewhat she performs if void of course, and be in Taurus, Cancer, Sagittarius or Pisces.” The Moon in the Via Combusta The “combust way”, from 15° Libra to 15° Scorpio. Lilly counsels care when the Moon is here. It is shorter than the others but contains Lilly's complete natal method, demonstrated on the worked nativity of “an English Merchant, born 19 September 1616.” Astronomy — verified Doctrine — historical Predictive validity — none demonstrated Open the Nativity Calculator → Open the Book III Master Tool → The Master Tool adds the timing methods below — profection & Lord of the Year, hyleg & alcocoden, primary directions and the solar return — all computed. Lilly's natal method, in order 1 · Rectification Correcting an uncertain birth time. Lilly gives the Trutine of Hermes and Ptolemy's Animodar , but trusts most the rectification by accidents — fitting the chart to known events of the life. 2 · The Hyleg (giver of life) The “prorogator of life”: the Sun by day or Moon by night, if in a hylegiacal place (1st, 7th, 9th, 10th or 11th); otherwise the Ascendant. The Anareta is the planet that, by direction, threatens it. 3 · The Alcocoden (giver of years) The planet with most dignity over the hyleg's place; by its greater, mean or least years (tabulated in Book I) it indicates the possible length of life. Lilly leans far more on directing the hyleg than on a literal year-count. 4 · The Lord of the Geniture The planet with most essential and accidental dignity, best placed and most elevated — “the most powerful and predominant” in the figure, colouring the whole life. The calculator finds it for you. 5 · Temperament & manners The humoral balance (choleric / melancholic / sanguine / phlegmatic) from the Ascendant, its lord, the Moon, the season and the Lord of the Geniture; and the “manners”

docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Hermetic Chronology › Alchemy The Great Work · Companion to the Chronology Alchemy, the magnum opus Alchemy was never one thing. It was a real laboratory craft aimed at making gold, a branch of Hermetic philosophy, and — much later — a language psychologists borrowed for the transformation of the self. The transmutation of lead into gold was never achieved, yet the patient work of the furnace gave birth to real chemistry. This page keeps those layers distinct. Three layers, often confused Historians such as Lawrence Principe stress that historical alchemy was overwhelmingly material : practitioners in furnaces pursuing chrysopoeia — the making of gold — with genuine chemical skill, inside a Hermetic and Christian worldview. A second, spiritual reading was real for some adepts, who saw the work as a mirror of the soul's purification. A third, purely psychological reading is largely a twentieth-century overlay, popularised by C. G. Jung . All three appear below, labelled — because collapsing them into one is the most common error in popular accounts. The four stages of the Work Nigredo — The Blackening Nigredo The first and hardest phase. The prima materia is broken down — putrefaction, calcination, dissolution — until the matter turns uniformly black. The Rosarium Philosophorum (1550) describes this total blackening as the necessary beginning: nothing can transform until it has first decomposed. In the furnace: decomposition and charring of the base matter. The Jungian reading (20th-c. overlay): the confrontation with the shadow; crisis, grief, the collapse of an old identity. docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Hermetic Chronology › Field Guide Materials · Purpose · Timing A field guide to practice reported folklore Contemporary practitioners organise their craft by three things: the material they use, the purpose they intend, and how long they expect to wait for a result. This page maps that — drawn from what witchcraft and pagan communities actually say online and in conversation. It is an ethnographic snapshot of belief and practice, not a claim that any of it produces supernatural effects. Read this as folklore, not physics Everything below is what practitioner communities report — colours, stones, and herbs paired with intentions, and the timeframes people say to expect. Notably, practitioners themselves usually locate the power in focus and intention: "the power comes from the symbolism and purpose behind the ritual, rather than the object." That maps cleanly onto known psychology — attention, expectancy, ritual structure — which is why a practice can feel effective without any supernatural mechanism being involved. Correspondences also vary by tradition and source; treat them as a shared vocabulary, not fixed law. Safety first — especially with herbs Many plants are not safe to ingest, apply to skin, or burn in an enclosed space — rue, wormwood, tansy, and pennyroyal are toxic, and mugwort is contraindicated in pregnancy. Use herbs symbolically (sachets, charms, altar pieces), research toxicity before any other use, keep flames attended, and treat nothing here as medical, herbal, or psychological advice. docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Hermetic Chronology › Hermetica The genuine contemplative core · Companion to the Chronology The practice of the Hermetica The meditation techniques, rites, and hymns actually preserved in the ancient Hermetic texts — the Corpus Hermeticum, the Asclepius, and the Coptic Hermetica from Nag Hammadi. Their single aim is gnosis: rebirth, the soul's ascent, and union with the divine Mind. This is the real thing the modern "manifestation" tradition borrows its vocabulary from — and it is older, stranger, and far more demanding. How to read these steps The Hermetica are dialogues, not instruction manuals. Where a text gives an explicit instruction — for example, to sing a hymn outdoors facing the dawn — it's marked as such. Everywhere else, the steps below are a faithful reconstruction of the practice's shape from the narrative, and translations differ on detail. These are genuine ancient practices (Roman Egypt, 2nd–3rd century), aimed at knowing and uniting with God, not at producing outcomes or altering events. Read them as the architecture of a contemplative path, with sources to go to the originals. I · Corpus Hermeticum XIII The Way of Rebirth (palingenesis) The central transformation rite: to be "born again" in Mind (Nous) — the old, material self dissolved and a new self reconstituted out of the divine Powers. Hermes delivers it to his son Tat on a mountain. The practice: Prepare and resolve. Tat first steadies his purpose "against the deceit of the cosmos." The work begins with a deliberate turning away from the material self and a readiness to be remade. Name the twelve tormentors. docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Hermetic Chronology ☿ The Hermetic Chronology — as above, so below The maxim above, paraphrased from the Emerald Tablet , became the signature of a tradition that crossed languages and empires — from the star-priests of Babylon and the scribes of Egypt, through Roman Alexandria and the Arabic translators of Harran, to Ficino's Florence, John Dee's angelic tables, and the magical orders of the modern age. This timeline follows that documented thread, and marks where popular history shades into conspiracy. Documented history · labelled No demonstrated validity Honest note — please read This wing is the history of ideas, not an endorsement : Hermetic philosophy, alchemy and ritual magic are presented as historical symbolic systems with no demonstrated validity — described, never prescribed. Its promise is the epistemic label on every entry: documented history is kept visibly distinct from disputed readings, debunked claims, and conspiracy narratives that have no evidentiary basis. Every entry carries its source; day-datable moments can be cast as charts in the Workbench , with the Julian→Gregorian conversion and the era's accuracy tier shown. See the whole map. This timeline follows one tradition in order, top to bottom; its sister wing, The Great Confluence , lays nine traditions side by side on one era-warped time axis and draws the documented arcs where their books crossed languages and borders — from the Hermetica and the Picatrix to the Upaniṣads in Persian and the Secret of the Golden Flower in Jung's Zürich. Chronology reads the story; Confluence sees the map. The four labels documented Established by primary texts or peer-reviewed scholarship. disputed Contested among scholars, or popular but not settled. debunked A specific claim disproven

docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Hermetic Chronology › Rituals Practitioner's starter set · Companion to the Chronology Starter practices The genuine entry-level rites of Western ceremonial magic, drawn from published sources. What they reliably do is real and well understood; what they do not do is something evidence can certify. Read them as a practitioner's first steps and a piece of living history. On efficacy, plainly These practices reliably focus attention, steady the nervous system, induce a calm or mildly altered state, and give shape to an intention — the mechanisms studied in research on meditation, ritual, and expectancy. Their supernatural efficacy is not something evidence can establish, so nothing here is promised to work as magic. All six are gentle and safe; none is a substitute for medical or mental-health care. 00 · Universal · "step zero" Grounding & centering Before any rite, practitioners settle the body and breath. Sit upright, relax each part of the body in turn, and breathe slowly — a common pattern is a four-count in, four-count hold, four-count out — until attention rests in the present. What it reliably does: slow, paced breathing engages the parasympathetic "rest" response, lowering arousal and sharpening focus. It is the contemplative baseline every later practice is built on. — Standard across contemplative traditions; cf. relaxation/meditation research. 01 · Golden Dawn · 1888 The Qabalistic Cross A short centering rite, structurally the Western cousin of crossing oneself, using Hebrew words mapped to the Tree of Life: Touch the forehead — Ateh ("unto Thee"). Touch the chest — Malkuth ("the Kingdom"). Touch the right shoulder — ve-Geburah ("and the Power"). Touch the left shoulder — ve-Gedulah ("and the Glory"). Clasp the hands at the chest — le-O

docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Great Confluence ✶ The Great Confluence — a map of books and their journeys Nine traditions, thirty-five centuries, one time axis. Every mark is a text, a person, an event, a translation or an institution; every arc is a documented journey — a book carried over a border, put into a new language, argued with, absorbed. This map plots influence, never validity : it shows that the Picatrix reached Latin, not that its talismans work; that the Upaniṣads reached Persian, not that Vedānta is physics. Who read whom is real history — and it is astonishing enough. Prefer the story told in order? Read the Hermetic Chronology . Documented transmission · labelled No demonstrated validity Honest note — please read Every entry and every arc carries an epistemic label : documented transmission is kept visibly distinct from disputed readings, debunked claims, and conspiracy narratives — which stay on the map, drawn faint, because the record of what people falsely believed is also history. Dates for ancient texts are scholarly estimates; the map draws that uncertainty as soft bands, not sharp points, and the timeline's scale stretches as it nears the present — each era band says by how much. The arcs mean “demonstrably read, rendered or answered,” per the citation on each — influence is the claim, and the only claim . How to read this map Lanes = traditions Columns run west of the spine to east. The gold-framed centre lane is the confluence — moments that belong to more than one tradition. A lane's colour is a finding aid; the label is the fact. Shapes = kind ▮ text · ● person · ◆ event · ◎ translation · ⌂ institution. Shape carries the kind; colour carries the epistemic label — two separate channels, each also written in the drawer. Marks = certainty A crisp mark

docs.h: The astrological marriage experiment — and its honest re-run · What Jung did — the figures · The re-run — what chance alone produces · What Jung took from the failure
docs.b: Home › Traditions › Jung & astrology › The experiment The astrological marriage experiment — and its honest re-run For the Synchronicity essay (1952) Jung mounted the one formal empirical test of astrology he ever ran: collect the horoscopes of hundreds of married couples , tally the cross-aspects between the spouses' charts, and ask whether the tradition's "marriage aspects" — above all the Sun–Moon conjunction , astrology's own image of the marital coniunctio — occur more often between real spouses than chance allows. The three classic aspects did top his three batches in turn. The statistics, honestly done, showed nothing. The honest headline Jung's best batch rate was about 10% . At his own 8° orb, chance pairing alone yields ≈ 9% — as the simulation below shows live, and as the physicist Markus Fierz's correction showed him. Jung conceded the point in print, and reinterpreted the run of lucky aspects as synchronicity — a meaningful coincidence involving the experimenters' own hopeful psyche — not as evidence that the stars arrange marriages. What Jung did — the figures The re-run — what chance alone produces ↻ Re-run 200 fresh chance batches What Jung took from the failure The experiment was designed to illustrate synchronicity , and its failure became its lesson. That the traditional marriage aspects — the Sun–Moon coniunctio above all — surfaced at the top of each successive batch struck Jung not as astral causation (the numbers forbade that) but as a meaningful coincidence keyed to the psychic situation of the experimenters themselves: a "secret mutual connivance," the archetype of the marriage-union constellating in the material just as the researchers hoped to find it. docs.b: Home › Traditions › Jung & astrology ☉ Jung & astrology — the psychologist among the horoscopes In 1911 C. G. Jung told Freud that his evenings were "taken up very largely with astrology" — that he was making horoscopic calculations "in order to find a clue to the core of psychological truth." For the next fifty years astrology ran through his work: he cast charts for patients in difficult diagnoses, read the zodiac as the projected psychology of antiquity , hung the argument of Aion on the precession of the equinoxes , and staked his theory of synchronicity on a statistical marriage experiment with 483 couples' horoscopes — an experiment that failed, as he himself admitted, and which taught him something stranger than astrology instead. History of ideas · cited No demonstrated validity Honest note — please read Jung used astrology; he did not validate it. His considered position was that astrology works — when it seems to — as projected psychology and synchronicity , not as a causal science; his one formal test found nothing beyond chance, and he said so in print. This section reconstructs what he actually did, wrote and calculated, with citations throughout — the history of ideas, described never prescribed. This is the map: the primary Collected Works (with exactly where astrology appears in each), the key dated letters, and the secondary literature — including the indispensable one-volume collection of all Jung's astrology writing. A shortcut If you read one book: Safron Rossi & Keiron Le Grice (eds.), Jung on Astrology (Routledge, 2017) — it gathers every passage on astrology from across the Collected Works, the seminars and the letters into a single annotated volume. For the critical scholarship: Maggie Hyde, Jung and Astrology (1992) . docs.h: Jung's theory of astrology — what he claimed, and what he did not · 1 · Origins — the clinician takes up the horoscope · 2 · Projection — astrology as the psychology of antiquity · 3 · Synchronicity — meaning, not cause · 4 · The causal temptation — and its refusal · 5 · Typology — the four elements and the four functions · 6 · The anima and the coniunctio — Sol weds Luna · 7 · Aion — the precession of the aeons · 8 · The honest verdict
docs.b: Home › Traditions › Jung & astrology › The theory Jung's theory of astrology — what he claimed, and what he did not Jung neither dismissed astrology as superstition nor embraced it as a science. He held a third, subtler position, worked out over fifty years: that the horoscope is a mirror of the psyche — the projected "psychological knowledge of antiquity" — whose apparent hits, where they occur, are matters of meaning (synchronicity) rather than cause . This page lays out that theory in his own terms, cited throughout, and ends where he ended: with an honest verdict. Honest note Reconstructing Jung's ideas is not endorsing astrology, which has no demonstrated validity. Jung's own considered view was that astrology is not a causal science; his one formal test came out null and he said so (see the experiment ). What follows is the history of a great psychologist's thought. 1 · Origins — the clinician takes up the horoscope Jung's engagement began early and practically. In 1911, in the thick of his correspondence with Freud, he confided that his evenings were "taken up very largely with astrology," that he was "making horoscopic calculations in order to find a clue to the core of psychological truth," and — half-defending, half-confessing — that the occult sciences were a treasury of buried psychology worth excavating (the Freud/Jung Letters, 1911) . This was never idle: for the rest of his career he cast horoscopes clinically . He told the Indian astrologer B. V. Raman in 1947 that in cases of difficult psychological diagnosis he "usually" obtained a horoscope "in order to have a further point of view from an entirely different angle," and that the astrological data had more than once illuminated a case he could not otherwise unravel (Letters vol. 1, to B. docs.b: Home › Traditions › Kabbalah Kabbalah — the Tree of Life explorer & gematria The kabbalistic Tree of Life as Athanasius Kircher drew it ( Oedipus Aegyptiacus , 1652–54) and the Golden Dawn adopted it: ten sephiroth on three pillars, joined by twenty-two paths , one for each Hebrew letter. Click any sphere or path to open its record — sphere, pillar, planet (and its kamea ), Hebrew letter, Sefer Yetzirah letter-class, Golden Dawn tarot trump — and use the gematria calculator below to sum any Hebrew word the way the tradition did. Honest note — please read Everything on this page is a layered historical construction of no demonstrated validity , and each layer is dated: the Sefer Yetzirah (dating contested — mainstream c. 2nd–6th c. CE, proposals 2nd–9th) speaks of ten sefirot belimah as abstract numbers only; the named emanations are medieval Kabbalah (Bahir c. 1176, Zohar c. 1286); the earliest printed tree (1516) had only 17 paths ; the 22-path layout here is Kircher's (1652–54) ; the tarot–letter linkage begins with Lévi (1854–56 , with a different scheme); the Golden Dawn table is c. 1887–1900; Crowley edited it in 1904 and 1944. The gematria arithmetic is exact and checkable; what the tradition read into equal sums is symbolism, recorded for study — described, never prescribed. Contested values are flagged where they occur and never resolved . It reports only what the tradition computes — it does NOT measure compatibility, does NOT predict a marriage, and is NOT advice about any real relationship . The gendered "groom/bride" roles and the traditional "consequences" of a rajju or nāḍī match are recorded as the tradition's own claims , presented as belief, never as fact about people. Like all astrology it has no demonstrated validity . Described, never prescribed. If you are making a real decision about a relationship, nothing here should inform it. See Sources & Science . Guṇa-milāpa compares the two Moons : from each Moon's nakṣatra and rāśi the North scores eight kūṭas to a total of 36 (varṇa 1, vaśya 2, tārā 3, yoni 4, graha-maitrī 5, gaṇa 6, bhakūṭa 7, nāḍī 8), while the Tamil South checks ten poruthams with rajju and vedhā as vetoes. This lays both out with every rule cited and every genuine source-disagreement flagged ( ⚑ ) rather than silently resolved — including the one place the two systems flatly contradict each other (the 5/9 rāśi relation). Eight modules, each a short numbered path of lessons: read the page, drive the tool, check yourself, tick it off. The mathematics is first-class throughout : every calculation-bearing lesson states the actual formula or arithmetic, pulled from this site's own engine and verified against its pages. Your progress is saved on this device only . Read this first — the honest frame is lesson one Astrology has no demonstrated predictive validity and is classed by science as a pseudoscience (see Sources & Science ). This curriculum teaches you to compute and read a historical tradition — what astrologers and magicians believed and how they reasoned. The astronomy and the arithmetic are real and reproducible ; the interpretations are the tradition's, described, never prescribed . Magical and ritual material is recorded as history only, never as instruction. ✶ Why the math comes first In 1570 John Dee prefaced the first English Euclid with a case that the whole art rests on number. He filed astrology itself under the mathematical arts: "Astrologie is an Arte Mathematicall, which reasonably demonstrateth the operations and effectes of the naturall beames of light, and secrete influence of the Sterres and Planets…" "O comfortable allurement, O ravishing perswasion, to deale with a Science whose Subiect is so Auncient, so pure, so excellent, so surmounting all creatures…" — John Dee, Mathematicall Praeface to Billingsley's Euclid (London, 1570); public domain, Project Gutenberg #22062. docs.b: Home › Traditions › Muhūrta Muhūrta — the Indian election of the moment Astronomy — verified Doctrine — historical Predictive validity — none demonstrated Muhūrta is Indian astrology's electional art — the doctrine of choosing the moment . The day from sunrise to the next sunrise is cut into 30 muhūrtas (15 by day, 15 by night, each ≈48 minutes), each named and graded by the tradition — from inauspicious Rudra at sunrise to Samudra closing the night, with the famous Abhijit straddling local apparent noon and the pre-dawn Brāhma muhūrta of daily practice. Over this clock the pañcāṅga lays its screens: three avoided kālas each day (Rāhu-kāla, Yamaghaṇṭa, Gulika-kāla — one eighth of the daylight arc, picked by the weekday), and the classical quality gradings of the moment's tithi , yoga , karaṇa and nakṣatra . Honest note — please read Muhūrta is a historical electional doctrine of no demonstrated validity . The arithmetic on this page is real and reproducible — equal divisions of the measured sunrise/sunset arcs on a verified astronomical engine — but the gradings (auspicious, inauspicious, avoid) are the tradition's, reproduced faithfully for study with their sources; they are never a prediction or advice. Where the sources genuinely disagree (two muhūrta qualities, the Abhijit weekday exception, one table's misprint) the dispute is flagged ⚑ with both positions, never silently resolved . docs.b: Home › Traditions › Picatrix › Planetary Correspondences Planetary Magic — Correspondences In the Picatrix and Cornelius Agrippa, each of the seven planets governs particular petitions and is approached with its matching suffumigations, colours, metals, stones and named spirits. This is the raw material of talismanic election — the table from which a medieval or Renaissance practitioner would have chosen what to burn, wear and inscribe under a given planet. Honest note This page records historical magical practice for study — it is described, never prescribed. The correspondences are reproduced as the sources give them; astrology and talismanic magic have no demonstrated validity. Some historical recipes name substances that are toxic or illegal (for example opium, or "blood" in martial recipes); these are listed because the texts list them, and are in no way recommended. See Sources & Science . Disclaimer The seven planets Listed in Chaldean order (slowest to fastest). Each card gives the planet's petitions and its talismanic correspondences, with the spirit-name systems kept separate (see the note below). Why the spirit names are not merged The spirit-names come from different source-systems that must not be conflated. This reference shows two of them and keeps them apart; a third is noted but not yet included: System 1 — the Picatrix prayer-angel ( Picatrix Book III.7): the angel addressed in the planetary prayer (the Sun has none; it is approached directly). System 2 — Agrippa's triad ( Three Books of Occult Philosophy , Book II): a single system that names, for each planet, an Angel , its Intelligence (the ordering, benevolent power) and its Spirit (the "demon" name). docs.b: Home › Traditions › Picatrix › Kameas & sigils The planetary kameas — seven magic squares & their sigils Agrippa's "tables of the planets" ( Three Books of Occult Philosophy II.22): seven magic squares , from Saturn's 3×3 to the Moon's 9×9, each cell-pattern summing alike in every row, column and diagonal — real, checkable mathematics that the tradition read as the planets' "divine numbers." From these squares the magicians derived the planetary seals and traced the sigils of spirit-names — a line drawn cell to cell through a name's letter-values, begun with a circle and ended with a bar. Type any name below and watch the method work. Honest note — please read The mathematics is real — this page verifies every square live, and the engine's test suite proves all seven. The magic is historical practice of no demonstrated efficacy : the talismanic uses quoted below are Agrippa's own words, recorded for study and never a recommendation. The 1651 printing's errors (Kedemel's misprinted gematria, the Moon's corrupt Hebrew) are flagged rather than silently repaired — that is what honest sourcing looks like. The square Trace a name as a sigil (optional) Letter-values How the tradition did it Agrippa prints the finished seals and characters beside each square but withholds the method — "the wise searcher … shall easily find out." The key he leaves is Book III ch. 30: the Hebrew alphabet laid on the nine chambers of aiq bekar (units 1–9, tens 10–90, hundreds 100–900), so any name becomes a run of numbers; values beyond the square's range collapse to their chamber (300 → 3), and the line passes cell to cell — a circle where it begins, a bar where it ends, a small wave where a cell repeats. The praśna lagna stands for the querent and is judged exactly as a birth lagna (Praśna Mārga I.47); the Moon is the querent's mind and its house is itself a testimony; the Moon's nakṣatra is a data layer with no Western analogue. Layered on top is KP (Krishnamurti Paddhati) : each nakṣatra cut into nine subs proportional to the Vimśottarī years — a self-verifiable table of exactly 249 entries , from which the querent's chosen horary number fixes the ascendant. The arithmetic here is real and reproducible — the sidereal chart runs on the same verified engine as every other tool, and the 249-entry sub-lord table is generated from first principles and checks itself (exactly 249 entries, exactly 6 rāśi-boundary splits, arcs that tile the zodiac without gap or overlap). docs.b: Home › Traditions › Rasaśāstra & yantras Rasaśāstra & yantra-mathematics — the mercury alchemy and the magic squares of India Two Indian threads meet here. Rasaśāstra is the medieval alchemy of mercury (pārada): a dated chain of tantric texts that claimed mercury, refined through eighteen saṃskāras in a workshop of named yantras (apparatus), could turn base metal to gold ( lohavedha ) and perfect the body toward immortality ( dehavedha ). It did neither — but its mercury–sulphur chemistry is a real chapter in the history of chemistry. The second thread is yantra-mathematics : the nine navagraha magic squares , the dated classical squares (Varāhamihira's, the Khajuraho Chautisa ), the kaṭapayādi letter-numbers, and the 81-cell Sarvatobhadra Chakra — provable arithmetic, its provenance flagged honestly. Honest note — please read These are historical symbolic systems of no demonstrated validity — described here for study, never prescribed. The mathematics is real : every magic square on this page is verified live in your browser and proven again in the engine's test suite. The readings — planetary yantras, the Chakra's vedha — are the tradition's, reproduced with their sources and never a claim about the world. Provenance is flagged: the nine navagraha squares are the modern printed ("bazaar") tradition , kept separate from the dated classical squares; the Sarvatobhadra second ring rests on a single degraded source and is flagged ⚑ accordingly. The Nāgārjuna authorship, the text datings, and the Chakra's rules are contested — both positions kept, never resolved . ⚠ Toxicity — read before anything else The substances named below — mercury and its compounds (cinnabar/HgS, mercury chloride), lead , arsenic , orpiment and realgar, and their calcined "bhasmas" — are poisons . docs.b: Home › Oracles › Runes Elder Futhark Runes — cast & read The Elder Futhark is the oldest runic alphabet — 24 staves in three groups of eight (the ættir ), used to write the Germanic languages roughly c. 150–700 CE . Here the tradition's lot-casting is reproduced for study: scatter the lots in the manner Tacitus described, or draw a single rune or a three-rune spread. Each stave shows its attested medieval rune-poem stanza above its clearly-flagged modern keyword — never blended. Honest note — please read The Elder Futhark was first of all a writing system . Magical use is attested in inscriptions (charm-words like alu and laukaz ), but no ancient or medieval source gives a rune-by-rune divinatory meaning table — the neat "each rune = one meaning" oracle is a 20th-century construction (Ralph Blum, 1982). The three rune poems are attested; the stanza gists here are original paraphrases after Bruce Dickins (1915, public domain) , not a copyrighted modern translation. A historical divinatory art of no demonstrated validity . The lots are drawn by your browser's random generator; nothing leaves the page. No blank rune ships here (see the framing note below). docs.b: Home › Cast › Synastry (two charts) Synastry — the chart-to-chart aspect grid Synastry compares two nativities: the aspects one person's planets make to the other's, and where each person's planets fall in the other's houses. Both Catherine Urban (multi-chart "family dynamics" work) and Patrick Watson (who tutors synastry) practise it. This lays the full cross-aspect grid — person A down the side, person B across the top — with Lilly's moieties, applying/separating and the whole-sign aspect, then the house overlays both ways . The classical kernel is Ptolemy's rule that the two charts' luminaries in harmonious aspect make a lasting marriage; the full planet-by-planet grid is a modern convention, flagged as such. Honest note — please read Synastry has no demonstrated validity . The arithmetic is real — the same aspect engine, orbs and house cusps as every other tool — but the meaning read into a cross-aspect is a symbolic convention, not a measure of any relationship. It is Sanjay Rath's Odiśā-tradition annual chart ( srath.com ); P.V.R. Narasimha Rao refined it to match the soli-lunar month instead, and Jagannath Hora offers both with solar months as the shipped default — the default here. Honest note — please read Unlike almost everything else on this site, tithi-praveśa has no classical BPHS or Jaimini textual anchor : it is a modern lineage teaching (Rath attributes it to his Odiśā tradition; PVR Narasimha Rao refined it). The astronomy is exact and verifiable — the elongation match is ayanāṁśa-independent — but the technique's authority rests on a living school, not a cited verse, and the solar-vs-soli-lunar month choice is a genuine dispute (Rao himself cautions neither convention always wins). Rath's school also prefers a non-Lahiri ayanāṁśa (Puṣya-pakṣa/SSS); this engine stays on Lahiri, and only the month-window edges depend on that. Every one of these forks is shown, never silently resolved. It has no demonstrated validity — described, never prescribed. See Sources & Science . A historical philosophy/practice text presented for study — the siddhi (power) claims are the text

The editions disagree · Search every sūtra · Read the wing · Samādhi-pāda — on concentration · Sādhana-pāda — the means · Vibhūti-pāda — the powers · Kaivalya-pāda — isolation · The eight limbs, honestly · The editions & the method
docs.b: Home › Traditions › Yoga Sūtras ॐ The Yoga Sūtras of Patañjali — the study wing The Pātañjala Yoga-sūtra is a compact treatise of aphorisms — 196 of them in the inclusive count — that defines yoga in its second line as citta-vṛtti-nirodha , "the restriction of the fluctuations of mind-stuff", and then lays out a path to that stilling: the five kleśas to be thinned, the eight limbs ( aṣṭāṅga ) to be practised, the catalogue of powers ( vibhūti ) that arise on the way — and the warning that those powers are obstacles , not the goal. This wing renders every sūtra word-by-word from public-domain scholarship — each with a gist of the classical Vyāsa-bhāṣya (after Woods 1914) collapsible beneath its translation — and studies the text honestly: what it says, where its editions disagree, and where later tradition put words in its mouth. Historical text · studied closely No demonstrated validity Honest note — please read This is a wing over a historical philosophy and practice text , presented for study . The Yoga Sūtras describe a contemplative discipline and a metaphysics (Sāṃkhya-derived: puruṣa and prakṛti , the guṇas , kaivalya ); Book III catalogues siddhis — supernormal "powers" said to follow from saṃyama (knowledge of past and future, of others' minds, invisibility, levitation, and the rest). Those claims are the text's own, described here never prescribed . Patañjali himself calls the powers "obstacles to samādhi " (III.37) — a counterweight this wing keeps in view. Nothing here has demonstrated validity as a claim about the world; it is the history of an idea, read closely. docs.h: Samādhi-pāda — Book I, on concentration (51 sūtras)
docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Yoga Sūtras › Book I Samādhi-pāda — Book I, on concentration (51 sūtras) The first book defines the whole subject and its goal. Yoga is citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ — the restriction of the fluctuations of mind-stuff (I.2); the five fluctuations are named and analysed; the twin means are practice ( abhyāsa ) and dispassion ( vairāgya ); devotion to the Īśvara is offered as an aid; the obstacles are catalogued; and the book climbs the ladder of samāpatti (the balanced states) to seedless concentration ( nirbīja-samādhi ). Every sūtra below carries its Devanāgarī, IAST, a word-by-word gloss, the translation, and any editorial note. docs.h: Sādhana-pāda — Book II, the means (55 sūtras)
docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Yoga Sūtras › Book II Sādhana-pāda — Book II, the means (55 sūtras) The second book is practical. It opens with kriyā-yoga (self-discipline, self-study, devotion — II.1), diagnoses the five kleśas (afflictions) rooted in avidyā , sets out the relation of the seer and the seen , and then lists the eight limbs ( aṣṭāṅga , II.29) and works through the first five: yama , niyama , āsana , prāṇāyāma , pratyāhāra . The famous posture sūtra II.46 — sthira-sukham āsanam — is here, and it names no posture at all; the named postures belong to the commentary (see the āsana honesty layer ). The "grounding" effects of the yamas and niyamas (II.35–45) are the text's own claims, described not prescribed. ← Book I · Samādhi-pāda Book III · Vibhūti-pāda → ← Book I · Samādhi-pāda Book III · Vibhūti-pāda → Glosses after Rama Prasada (1912, SBH IV); translations after J. H. Woods (1914, HOS 17); Devanāgarī from sanskritdocuments.org with the Vedic accents stripped. See Sources .

docs.d: Book III of the Yoga Sūtras, the Vibhūti-pāda (
docs.h: Vibhūti-pāda — Book III, the powers (55 / 56 sūtras)
docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Yoga Sūtras › Book III Vibhūti-pāda — Book III, the powers (55 / 56 sūtras) The third book completes the eight limbs — dhāraṇā , dhyāna , samādhi (III.1–3) — and fuses the three into saṃyama (III.4), the instrument of every vibhūti (power) that follows: from knowledge of past and future, of the cries of all beings, of others' minds, through invisibility, foreknowledge of death, mastery of the elements and the senses, up to omniscience. Then the pivot: III.37 declares that these powers are obstacles to concentration — perfections only from the standpoint of the outgoing mind — and the book ends turning toward kaivalya . The siddhis are the text's own claims, described never prescribed. Dual numbering — read this Book III is the one book whose sūtra count is disputed. The inclusive editions (Taimni, Satchidananda, Iyengar) count a sūtra "etena śabdādyantardhānam uktam" ("by this the disappearance of sound & the rest is explained") after the invisibility sūtra, giving 56 sūtras and the whole text 196. The Vyāsa-bhāṣya vulgate — sanskritdocuments, Woods (1914), Rama Prasada (1912), Bhoja — omits it, giving 55 and 195. Below, that record is badged as the variant , and the offset it causes (every later sūtra shifts by one) is why the two numbering schemes diverge from it onward. Use the toggle to switch the displayed numbers; the record itself never moves. ← Book II · Sādhana-pāda Book IV · Kaivalya-pāda → 196 · inclusive 195 · vulgate ← Book II · Sādhana-pāda Book IV · Kaivalya-pāda → Glosses after Rama Prasada (1912, SBH IV, mapped by content — his Book III has 54 numbered sūtras); translations after J. H. Woods (1914, HOS 17); Devanāgarī from sanskritdocuments.org with the Vedic accents stripped. See Sources .

docs.h: Kaivalya-pāda — Book IV, isolation (34 sūtras)
docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Yoga Sūtras › Book IV Kaivalya-pāda — Book IV, isolation (34 sūtras) The final book is the most philosophical. It opens by tracing the siddhis to five sources (birth, herbs, mantra, austerity, concentration — IV.1), then argues, against the Buddhist idealists, for the reality of the external object and the immutable seer ( puruṣa ) who is not the mind; it follows the mind as it "slopes toward kaivalya ", reaches the dharma-megha ("rain-cloud of dharma") concentration, and defines kaivalya — Isolation — in the closing sūtra as the guṇas resolved back to their source, or the power of consciousness grounded in itself. Bhoja's numbering Bhoja's Rājamārtaṇḍa treats the vulgate IV.16 ("na caikacittatantraṃ vastu…", the anti-idealist sūtra) as commentary rather than a sūtra, so his Book IV has 33 sūtras and his numbers run one behind the vulgate from IV.17 onward. Records below carry a Bhoja IV.n badge where his number differs, and IV.16 is flagged as absent in his edition. ← Book III · Vibhūti-pāda The eight limbs, honestly → ← Book III · Vibhūti-pāda The eight limbs, honestly → Glosses after Rāma Prasāda (1912, SBH IV); translations after J. H. Woods (1914, HOS 17); Devanāgarī from sanskritdocuments.org with the Vedic accents stripped. Two reading-variants (IV.16, IV.34) are flagged in-record. See Sources .

docs.d: The theory of the Yoga Sūtras: the eight-limb (aṣṭāṅga) map of II.29 limb-by-limb with linked sūtra spans; the yamas and niyamas; the āsana honesty layer — the sūtras name NO postures, the Vyāsa-bhāṣya names about eleven (count ambiguous), with corrected Woods renderings and modern illustrative line-diagrams for five; the vibhūti (powers) catalogue framed by III.37

docs.b: Home › Traditions › The Yoga Sūtras › Theory The eight limbs, the āsana honesty layer & the powers This page reads the architecture of the text: the eight limbs ( aṣṭāṅga ) declared in II.29 and where each is defined; the yamas and niyamas ; the āsana honesty layer — the sūtras name no postures at all, and the familiar posture-names come from the commentary; the catalogue of powers ( vibhūti ) with the text's own warning attached; and kaivalya , the goal beyond every power. The honest frame, in one line Everything below is the claim of a historical text and its commentaries , described never prescribed — the postures, the breath-work and above all the siddhis (powers) are what the tradition says , presented for study, with no demonstrated validity as a claim about the world. The eight limbs (aṣṭāṅga) — the map of II.29 II.29 lists the eight aṅga — "limbs" or "aids", not a strict ladder — that carry the practitioner toward discriminative discernment. The last three, practised together on one object, become saṃyama ( III.4 ); III.7 calls the inner three "direct aids", yet III.8 calls even them "outer" relative to seedless samādhi — the text relativises its own scheme. # Limb What it is Defined at Effects / development 1 yama the five abstentions II.30 – 31 II.33–34 (cultivate the opposite); II.35–39 (their "grounding" effects) 2 niyama the five observances II.32 II.40–45 3 āsana seat / posture — "stable and easy" II.46 II.47–48 (names no posture — see below) 4 prāṇāyāma regulation of the breath II.49 II.50–53 5 pratyāhāra withdrawal of the senses II.54 II.55 6 dhāraṇā fixed attention (binding the mind to a place) III.1 the three together = saṃyama ( III.4–8 ): inner aids (III.7), yet outer to seedless samādhi (III.8) 7 dhyāna contemplation (the unbroken flow of one idea)
